skill.name: stripe-integration
skill.description: Guides implementation of Stripe checkout, subscriptions, webhooks, refunds, customer records, and test payment flows.
skill.summary: Build Stripe checkout, subscription, webhook, refund, and customer-management flows.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution, reconnaissance, weak-crypto, and critical combination alerts are false positives from Markdown code blocks and Stripe terminology. No prompt injection or malicious exfiltration was found. The real concern is that examples show secret values assigned in source code, which could encourage unsafe Stripe key handling.

medium_findings.title: Hardcoded Stripe Secret Placeholders in Examples
medium_findings.description: The examples assign Stripe API and webhook secrets directly in source code. The values are placeholders, but copied patterns could lead users to commit real payment secrets.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines show direct secret assignment patterns in executable examples. The values are placeholders, so this is unsafe guidance rather than evidence of active credential theft.

low_findings.title: Command Execution Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The backtick matches are Markdown inline code and fenced code blocks. I found no shell execution instructions or command injection pattern in those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation formatting, webhook event names, or Python code fences. They do not invoke a shell or execute external commands.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Alerts Are Placeholder Redirects
low_findings.description: The URL matches are example success, cancel, image, and return URLs for Stripe flows. They do not send secrets to an unexpected endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs use placeholder domains inside payment examples. There is no evidence of credential exfiltration or hidden network behavior.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alert Has No Supporting Evidence
low_findings.description: The flagged line is the skill description and does not name or implement a weak cryptographic algorithm. Later webhook code uses SHA-256 HMAC.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is descriptive metadata, not executable cryptographic code. No weak algorithm is present at that location.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The flagged lines reference Stripe payload fields, customer identifiers, and payment method identifiers. They do not collect host or system information.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The terms appear in Stripe domain objects and comments. I found no commands or APIs for enumerating the local system.

low_findings.title: Incomplete Manual Webhook Verification Example
low_findings.description: The manual HMAC helper omits Stripe signature header parsing details. Users should prefer Stripe Webhook.construct_event, which is also shown earlier.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The helper is clearly presented as manual verification, but it is simplified. The official construct_event example reduces the practical risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Secret Assignment in Source Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Stripe API keys and webhook secrets are represented as direct assignments. Production guidance should use environment variables or a secret manager.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is visible in multiple examples. The placeholders are not real credentials, but the implementation style is risky for payment systems.

dangerous_patterns.title: Networked Payment API Operations
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes examples that create sessions, payment intents, subscriptions, portal sessions, refunds, and disputes through Stripe APIs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The Stripe SDK operations are intentional for this skill. They are still sensitive because payment actions can affect customers and money.

content.value_statement: Payment integrations are difficult to implement securely across checkout, subscriptions, and webhooks. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured Stripe patterns for common payment workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains hosted Stripe Checkout and custom Payment Intent flows.
Shows subscription creation with saved payment methods and incomplete payment handling.
Provides webhook handling patterns with signature verification and event routing.
Covers customer records, payment methods, refunds, disputes, and billing portal sessions.
Includes Stripe test card scenarios for successful and failed payments.
limitations: Examples are instructional and require adaptation before production use.
The skill does not include a complete deployable application.
Secret management guidance is incomplete in several examples.
It focuses on Python and JavaScript examples, not every Stripe SDK.

use_cases.title: Launch hosted checkout
use_cases.description: Create a Stripe Checkout flow for one-time purchases or recurring subscriptions.

use_cases.title: Process billing webhooks
use_cases.description: Handle successful payments, failed payments, and subscription cancellations with verified webhook events.

use_cases.title: Manage customer billing
use_cases.description: Add customer records, attach payment methods, open billing portal sessions, and process refunds.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic checkout plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Stripe integration skill to outline a hosted checkout flow for a single product. Include backend steps, frontend redirects, and test cases.

prompt_templates.title: Add subscription billing
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Stripe integration skill to design subscription billing for my SaaS app. Include customer creation, price IDs, payment confirmation, and cancellation handling.

prompt_templates.title: Review webhook security
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Stripe integration skill to review my webhook design. Check signature verification, idempotency, event coverage, retries, and database update order.

prompt_templates.title: Design a marketplace payment flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Stripe integration skill to design a Stripe Connect marketplace flow. Include onboarding, platform fees, refunds, disputes, and operational monitoring.

output_examples.input: Plan a subscription checkout integration for a SaaS product.
output: A backend flow for customer creation, subscription setup, and payment confirmation.
A webhook event map for invoice and subscription lifecycle changes.
A test checklist using Stripe test mode and failure cases.

output_examples.input: Review a payment webhook endpoint before production.
output: A risk list covering signature checks, idempotency, retry behavior, and event ordering.
Recommended changes for secret storage and error handling.
A concise launch checklist for payment operations.

best_practices: Store Stripe API keys and webhook secrets in environment variables or a secret manager.
Verify every webhook signature and process events idempotently.
Test successful, declined, 3D Secure, refund, and subscription cancellation flows.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode live Stripe keys or webhook secrets in source files.
Do not rely only on client-side payment confirmation for fulfillment.
Do not store or process raw card data on your server.

faq.question: Does this skill create production-ready Stripe code?
faq.answer: It provides implementation patterns and examples. Production systems still need secure configuration, testing, and business-specific validation.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it cover subscriptions?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes subscription creation, saved payment methods, invoices, and subscription cancellation handling.

faq.question: Does it cover webhook security?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows signature verification and idempotent processing patterns, but secret handling should be improved.

faq.question: Does it handle refunds and disputes?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples for refunds and dispute evidence updates.

faq.question: What is the main security warning?
faq.answer: Several examples show secret placeholders assigned in source code. Use environment variables or secret managers instead.
